title: Manage Apps → Control Marketplace Access
excerpt:
Description

posts.author: ProjectMastermind (IngenuityKat)
posts.cookedHtml:
Description

Today, users can access and browse the marketplace even in environments where third-party apps are not allowed due to security and compliance policies.

Key limitations:
-No ability to disable or hide the marketplace for end users
-Users can still search for apps and submit install requests
-No distinction between third-party apps and Vibe (internal/native) apps in Manage Apps
-Creates confusion when users request apps that cannot be approved
-Adds unnecessary overhead for admins who must review and decline requests then follow up with a manual email explaining why.

In organizations with strict security policies, this creates friction and misalignment between platform capabilities and governance requirements.

What are you trying to achieve

We need to enforce governance at the platform level while still enabling internal innovation.

Specifically:
-Ability to disable or hide marketplace access for all or selected users
-Prevent users from searching, browsing, or requesting third-party apps
-Allow access to Vibe apps only (internal or approved solutions)
-Clearly separate approved/internal tools vs restricted external apps
-Reduce unnecessary install requests and admin overhead
-Align user experience with security and compliance policies

In our environment, third-party apps require a formal approval process that most users are not aware of. Ideally, users should not even see or attempt to install these apps unless they are explicitly allowed.

Exec question it fails: How do we enforce security policies without creating confusion or unnecessary admin work?

posts.author: kylecordes (Kyle Cordes)
posts.cookedHtml:
It’s a publicly viewable marketplace. Everyone on the entire internet can see it. Making it so logged in users get some block message will mostly just irritate them.

Part of doing restrictive governance is dealing with the downsides - and that includes having to tell people, sometimes executives they cannot have a valuable and useful addon because the organization has decided not categorically against add-ons.

posts.author: ProjectMastermind (IngenuityKat)
posts.cookedHtml:
While it’s technically available on the website, that’s not how users actually behave. They’re not going on a side quest to find it there—they’re clicking the very visible Marketplace icon inside the product.

That makes it immediately accessible and actionable, which is exactly why UI-level controls matter. Without them, discovery and install intent happen in the tool, while governance only kicks in after the fact—making it reactive instead of preventative.

posts.author: kylecordes (Kyle Cordes)
posts.cookedHtml:
Definitely would be more administrator-friendly if they could make that button disappear, especially if done in a way that does not result in an overt message about it being disabled. It’s a little bigger project than it seems though. Monday has various in-context recommendations that pop up for add-ons, as well as tooling searches (such as an automation) that sometimes bring up results from add-ons.

(And for the sake of transparency, as a marketplace app maker, I’m not excited about them hiding all these things. Discovery is already an immensely hard problem, and taking away the few remaining discovery points for some accounts results in less opportunity for makers.)

posts.author: ProjectMastermind (IngenuityKat)
posts.cookedHtml:
Kyle Cordes:

appear, especially if done in a way that does not result in an overt message about it being disabled. It’s a little bigger project than it seems though. Monday has various in-context recommendations that pop up for add-ons, as well as tooling searches (such as an automation) that sometimes bring up results from add-ons.

(And for the sake of transparency, as a marketplace app maker, I’m not excited about them hiding all these things. Discovery is already an immensely hard problem, and taking away the few remaining discovery points for some accounts results in less opportunity for makers.)

I mean, I get it from an app developer perspective, that makes complete sense. Discovery is already tough, and removing those surfaces doesn’t exactly help.

That said, from our side it’s a very different reality.

We literally cannot use marketplace apps due to cybersecurity restrictions—it’s a hard stop, not a “we haven’t gotten there yet.” And even if that weren’t the case, we’re operating at a scale (~60k employees) where:

  • There’s no centralized budget for apps

  • Every org has different funding structures

  • Managing ownership, approvals, and renewals across that many teams would be… a full-time chaos engine

So what happens in practice is users discover something, get excited, click in—and then immediately hit a wall. That creates more confusion than value.

And honestly, I don’t even know that this is purely a discovery problem.

A lot of it comes down to:

  • Unclear value prop in the moment

  • Positioning that doesn’t translate to enterprise use

  • Tools that feel built for team-level adoption, not locked-down enterprise environments

In our world, we’re not browsing for “cool add-ons.” We’re asking:

  • Is it cyber-approved?

  • Can it scale to tens of thousands of users?

  • Who owns, funds, and supports it?

If those answers aren’t obvious, it’s a non-starter—no matter how good the app is.

So it’s less about hiding discovery entirely, and more about not sending thousands of users down paths they fundamentally can’t take. Right now, that gap between what’s discoverable and what’s actually usable in an enterprise setting is where the real friction is.

posts.author: kylecordes (Kyle Cordes)
posts.cookedHtml:
We’re drifting off your original post topic, but it’s possibly very helpful for others to read too, and it circles back at the end, so…

Those are great points! From an app vendor point of view, it can be challenging to meet the requirements a large company reasonably has.

It’s partly a problem that should be solved by the platform itself. monday is making considerable ongoing strides here by improving “monday Code”, which can bring the entire hosting infrastructure within monday’s trust boundary, rather than customers having to evaluate their data being shuffled off to a different trust boundary. But monday Code is still limited in capabilities compared to the whole universe of hosting capabilities, and still mostly leaves account level multi-tenancy to the implementer instead of implementing and enforcing it at the monday Code platform level.

Even if that were solved, even on a popular platform like monday finding enough customers to have a successful business is a challenge. There is a highly “pyramid-shaped” distribution of the sizes of the candidate customers, and the discovery mechanisms reward popular apps - essentially making it necessary to start pursuing small customers then work up from there.

Small customers (like large ones) obviously have every reason to want their apps to be secure in design, implementation, and hosting. However, small customers generally won’t pay much extra for that vs an app lacking those things. Large customers, if they are willing to buy, will pay more for trustworthy implementation and hosting… but circling back to the original topic here, a disproportionate number of large customers have decided before even evaluating any apps that they won’t be buying.
